domain: ethnomusicology / vocal acoustics — Tuvan khoomei throat singing (biphonic simultaneous fundamental + selectively-amplified single overtone via formant-merging vocal-tract filter)
specific_mechanism: The singer produces a fundamental at the larynx (a complex harmonic source) AND simultaneously tunes the vocal tract — narrow tongue/lip configurations merge formants F2 and F3 to create a sharp filter peak at 1-2 kHz. The harmonic at the filter peak is dramatically amplified; the other harmonics are heard as the booming low. The output is biphonic: ONE low fundamental + ONE selected high overtone, both audible simultaneously. The key is the singular sharp resonance peak in the filter — the vocal tract is configured to be HIGHLY selective.
llm_application: Selective-overtone decoding via merged-head resonance filter: train two parallel decoding heads with strong shared backbone — head A produces a 'fundamental' answer (high-level conceptual summary) while head B produces a single sharp 'overtone' answer (one specific cited fact or formal statement). Force head B's attention pattern to merge two specific high-frequency-content heads (analog: F2+F3 merge), producing a single sharp filter peak in the answer space. The output is biphonic: simultaneously a conceptual summary + a single salient specific datum, both presented in the same response.
novelty_claim: Multi-head attention specialization is heavily studied (Voita 1905.09418, Michel 2019.09312, head-merging surveys). Dual decoder architectures exist (Vaswani 1706.03762 enc-dec, BART 1910.13461). None implements a biphonic 'fundamental + single selected overtone' pattern with deliberate head-merging to produce a sharp single overtone in the output channel.
